questions.stem: A 35-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being rescued from a house fire. He was unconscious at the scene. On arrival, he is drowsy but opening eyes to voice. Pulse 110, BP 100/60, SpO2 94% on 15L O2. He has soot in his nostrils and oropharynx. No visible burns. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The presence of soot and history of fire in a confined space strongly suggests inhalation injury. Carbon monoxide poisoning may coexist but the clinical picture of altered consciousness with soot points to direct thermal/chemical injury to airways. Cyanide poisoning is less common and usually presents with severe acidosis. Simple hypoxia and anaphylaxis are less likely given the context.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman sustained a 5% TBSA superficial partial thickness scald on her forearm from boiling water 3 days ago. She now presents with increasing pain, redness extending beyond the burn margin, and purulent discharge. She is febrile at 38.5°C. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The classic signs of infection (pain, erythema extending, purulence, fever) in a superficial burn that was healing point to cellulitis. Necrotising fasciitis would cause severe systemic toxicity, crepitus, and disproportionate pain. Deepening of burn would not cause spreading erythema. Contact dermatitis and allergic reaction would not typically cause purulent discharge or fever.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of gout is taking allopurinol. He presents with a 2-day history of painful blistering over his trunk and arms, with skin peeling in sheets. He denies any heat exposure. Nikolsky sign is positive. He has oral mucosal ulcers. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The rapid onset of widespread blistering with positive Nikolsky and mucosal involvement in an elderly patient on allopurinol is classic for toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN). Stevens-Johnson syndrome involves less than 10% body surface area detachment; here the extent suggests TEN. Erythema multiforme typically has target lesions. Burn is excluded by absence of heat exposure. Bullous pemphigoid usually occurs in older adults but is less acute and has large tense bullae without mucosal involvement.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old man with long-standing type 2 diabetes and peripheral neuropathy presents with a painless blister on his right heel. He recalls using a hot water bottle for warmth last night. On examination, the blister is tense with clear fluid and the underlying skin is white and insensate to pinprick. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The painless nature, white appearance, and lack of sensation indicate full thickness destruction of dermis and nerve endings, typical of a full thickness burn in a neuropathic patient. Diabetic foot ulcers usually occur on pressure points and are chronic; pressure sores occur over bony prominences in immobile patients. The history of hot water bottle supports burn. Superficial and deep partial thickness burns are painful.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man sustains a circumferential deep partial thickness burn to his left forearm from hot liquid. Over the next 6 hours, he complains of increasing pain despite analgesics, and his hand becomes pale, cold, and pulseless. Capillary refill is >5 seconds. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Circumferential burn leading to increased tissue pressure causes compartment syndrome, characterised by pain out of proportion, pallor, pulselessness, and delayed capillary refill. Necrotising fasciitis is infectious with systemic toxicity and crepitus. DVT would cause swelling but not pulselessness. Arterial occlusion is less likely without trauma. Burn depth progression would not cause acute pulselessness.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being rescued from a house fire. He has facial burns, singed nasal hairs, soot in the oropharynx, and a hoarse voice. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the presence of inhalation injury?
questions.explanation: Bronchoscopy allows direct visualization of airway mucosa and is the gold standard for diagnosing inhalation injury. COHb confirms carbon monoxide poisoning, not inhalation injury per se.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old electrician sustains a high-voltage electrical burn to his left arm. He has no visible entry or exit wound initially, but his urine is dark. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the presence of rhabdomyolysis?
questions.explanation: Serum CK is the most sensitive and specific marker of muscle injury; urine myoglobin can be falsely positive due to rapid clearance. CK correlates directly with the extent of muscle damage.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child presents with a scald injury to his anterior chest from hot water. The burn appears pale and does not blanch, but sensation is intact. Which investigation is most appropriate to determine burn depth?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends laser Doppler imaging for indeterminate depth burns as it measures skin perfusion and predicts healing potential. Biopsy is invasive and rarely needed.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with a full-thickness flame burn to his lower leg has a tense, swollen limb with pain on passive toe flexion and loss of palpable pulses. Which investigation is most appropriate to diagnose compartment syndrome?
questions.explanation: Direct intracompartmental pressure measurement is the gold standard; pressures >30 mmHg confirm compartment syndrome. Doppler ultrasound can detect flow but cannot rule out raised pressure.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with 40% total body surface area burns from a house fire is intubated and ventilated. An arterial blood gas shows: pH 7.08, pCO2 4.5 kPa, pO2 10 kPa, HCO3 12 mmol/L, lactate 14 mmol/L, carboxyhaemoglobin 25%. Which diagnosis is most likely in addition to the thermal injury?
questions.explanation: The combination of high lactate and high COHb is classic for cyanide toxicity; carbon monoxide alone does not cause such profound lactic acidosis. Cyanide inhibits oxidative phosphorylation, leading to anaerobic metabolism.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 25% total body surface area (TBSA) burn to his torso and arms sustained in a house fire. There is no clinical evidence of inhalation injury. He weighs 80 kg. Which fluid is most appropriate for initial intravenous resuscitation?
questions.explanation: In UK burns resuscitation, Hartmann's (lactated Ringer's) is the crystalloid of choice for the Parkland formula-based resuscitation. 0.9% sodium chloride can cause hyperchloraemic acidosis and is not preferred. Colloids are not used in initial resuscitation. Dextrose is not appropriate.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with a 5% TBSA deep partial thickness burn on her lower leg from a radiator. She has poor glycaemic control. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Admit for intravenous antibiotics and wound debridement on the ward
Apply a silver-impregnated dressing and discharge with oral analgesia
Start an intravenous insulin sliding scale
Refer to the regional burns unit for surgical assessment
Apply a simple non-adherent dressing and prescribe oral co-amoxiclav
questions.explanation: Deep partial thickness burns in elderly patients with comorbidity (diabetes) should be referred to a burns unit for specialist assessment, as early excision may be indicated. Silver dressings alone are inadequate; sliding scale addresses glycaemia but not the burn; simple dressing with antibiotics is not sufficient; debridement on a general ward is not appropriate.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with a 15% TBSA burn presents 3 days after injury with increasing wound pain, purulent discharge, and a temperature of 39°C. What is the most appropriate first-line antibiotic?
questions.explanation: In burn wound infection, typical pathogens include Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Empiric therapy should be broad-spectrum with adequate Gram-negative coverage; piperacillin-tazobactam is a common first-line choice. Flucloxacillin is too narrow; vancomycin covers MRSA but not gram-negatives; topical mupirocin is for superficial MRSA, not systemic infection.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency sustains a 3% TBSA superficial partial thickness burn on his hand. Which of the following is contraindicated in his management?
questions.explanation: Silver sulfadiazine is a sulfonamide derivative and can cause haemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. All other options are safe: hydrocolloid dressing, paracetamol, ibuprofen, and tetanus toxoid are not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with 40% TBSA full thickness burns from a chemical spill. He has stridor, soot in his sputum, and oxygen saturations of 88% on room air. What is the most appropriate initial therapy?
options: Apply flamazine (silver sulfadiazine) to all burns
Perform bedside escharotomy of the chest
Secure the airway with endotracheal intubation
Start intravenous Hartmann's solution at 500 mL/hour
questions.explanation: The presence of stridor indicates upper airway oedema from inhalation injury. Securing the airway via intubation is the priority before any other intervention. Oxygen can be given after intubation; fluid resuscitation, escharotomy (if needed), and topical therapy come after airway management.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old laboratory worker spills hydrofluoric acid on his left forearm. He washed it off with water immediately but still has intense pain. On examination, there is an erythematous patch with small blisters. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: For hydrofluoric acid burns, after copious irrigation, the specific treatment is topical calcium gluconate gel to bind fluoride ions and relieve pain. Intravenous calcium is reserved for severe cases with systemic toxicity. Silver sulfadiazine is not specific, debridement is not immediate, and analgesia alone does not address the chemical injury.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman sustains 30% total body surface area (TBSA) partial-thickness burns from a scald. She weighs 70 kg, heart rate 110, blood pressure 100/60. She has no other injuries. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Apply Flamazine cream to all burns.
Arrange urgent transfer to a burns unit.
Start intravenous fluid resuscitation with Hartmann's solution at 500 ml/hour.
questions.explanation: In major burns (>20% TBSA), fluid resuscitation is the urgent priority to prevent hypovolaemic shock. Using the Parkland formula (4 ml/kg/% TBSA), half the 24-hour total is given in the first 8 hours: 4 ml x 70 kg x 30% = 8400 ml; half = 4200 ml over 8 hours = 525 ml/h. Starting IV fluids at ~500 ml/h is correct. Catheter, analgesia, dressings, and transfer are important but secondary to initial fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with a history of heart failure has 15% TBSA burns to his legs from a hot water scald. He is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Administer intravenous furosemide to prevent fluid overload.
Start IV fluids according to the Parkland formula.
Apply silver sulfadiazine and dress the wounds.
Seek advice from the regional burns unit regarding fluid management.
questions.explanation: In elderly patients with cardiac comorbidities, standard Parkland formula can cause fluid overload. NICE guidelines recommend discussing with a burns specialist for individualised fluid resuscitation. Starting presumptive fluids or giving diuretics is wrong without specialist input. Dressing and echo are not the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being trapped in a smoke-filled room. He has soot around his mouth and nose, but his voice is clear, there is no stridor, and oxygen saturation is 97% on room air. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Give high-flow oxygen via a non-rebreather mask.
questions.explanation: In smoke inhalation, carbon monoxide poisoning is a common threat even without respiratory symptoms. Checking carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb) is essential to guide oxygen therapy. High-flow oxygen is indicated if COHb is elevated, but the immediate diagnostic step is to check the level. Bronchoscopy, antibiotics, and dressings are not immediately needed without signs of airway compromise or infection.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old cleaner splashes a strong alkaline drain cleaner into his left eye. He has severe pain, photophobia, and conjunctival injection. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Irrigate the eye with at least one litre of normal saline.
Evert the eyelids and remove any visible particles.
questions.explanation: For chemical eye burns, copious and continuous irrigation is the critical immediate step to dilute and remove the alkaline agent, which can cause progressive damage. While analgesia, ophthalmology referral, and eyelid eversion are important, irrigation takes priority and should be started without delay. Antibiotics are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man is rescued from a house fire. He has soot around his nose and mouth, singed nasal hairs, and is coughing up carbonaceous sputum. His voice is hoarse. Oxygen saturation is 94% on air. Which single finding is the strongest indicator that he is at immediate risk of life-threatening airway obstruction?
questions.explanation: Hoarseness indicates oedema at the level of the glottis, which can rapidly progress to stridor and complete airway obstruction. Other findings (soot, singed hairs, carbonaceous sputum) suggest inhalation injury but do not directly signal impending airway compromise. An oxygen saturation of 94% is not reassuring as it can drop rapidly.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old child presents with scalds to both hands and forearms. The carer gives a vague history of the child pulling a cup of hot tea off a table. Which clinical finding is the most concerning for non-accidental injury (NAI)?
options: Scald to palmar surface of hands
questions.explanation: A clear, well-demarcated upper border with uniform depth suggests the limb was forcibly held under hot water, classic for immersion abuse. In contrast, spill burns usually have irregular borders, splash marks, and varying depth. While a withdrawn child may raise concerns, the clear upper border is the most specific sign.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old industrial worker splashes a clear liquid on his right hand. He develops intense pain and within 30 minutes notices tingling around his mouth. The liquid is later identified as hydrofluoric acid. Which clinical finding is most indicative of life-threatening systemic toxicity?
questions.explanation: Perioral paraesthesia is an early sign of hypocalcaemia due to fluoride binding calcium. Hypocalcaemia can lead to tetany, seizures, and cardiac arrhythmias. Pain out of proportion is characteristic but local; nausea is non-specific. Blistering and erythema are local findings. Systemic toxicity is heralded by neurological symptoms.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man sustained a circumferential full-thickness burn to his left forearm from contact with a hot metal pipe 6 hours ago. Which clinical sign is the most reliable early indicator of compartment syndrome in this setting?
options: Pain on passive extension of fingers
Paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution
questions.explanation: Pain on passive stretch is the earliest and most sensitive sign of compartment syndrome. Later signs include paraesthesia (nerve ischaemia), pallor, and pulselessness (often a late and absent finding due to pressure rarely exceeding systolic BP). Tense swelling is present but not diagnostic alone.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with 40% TBSA full-thickness burns from a house fire is brought to the ED. He is shivering, confused, and his core temperature is 34.5°C. Which vital sign abnormality poses the greatest immediate threat to his survival?
questions.explanation: Hypothermia (temperature <35°C) in major burns worsens coagulopathy, acidosis, and metabolic demand, and is strongly associated with increased mortality. While hypotension, tachycardia, and low urine output are common and require intervention, hypothermia is a critical, often missed, insult that must be addressed immediately to prevent further deterioration.
